subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
title: Global Observatory on Water Investments (GOCWI)
focus: Global Water Partnership's initiative to mobilize financial momentum for water security.
rationale: A distinct international initiative focused on water investments and security, suitable for a standalone factual question.
sectionExcerpt: It forms a core pillar of the Global Water Partnership’s (GWP) Global Transformation Agenda on Water Investments. GWP is a multi-stakeholder action network and intergovernmental organisation dedicated to equitable, sustainable, and efficient management of water resources.
evidenceBullets: Forms a core pillar of the Global Water Partnership's (GWP) Global Transformation Agenda.
GWP is an intergovernmental organisation and multi-stakeholder network.
Aims to mobilise 15 billion for water security by 2030.
Serves as a high-level platform to sustain political and financial momentum for water investments.

subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
title: River Cities Alliance (RCA)
focus: Institutional framework and initiatives for river-sensitive urban planning in Indian cities.
rationale: A major national initiative involving multiple ministries and specific frameworks like the Urban River Management Plans (URMP), highly relevant for UPSC governance and environment sections.
sectionExcerpt: Joint initiative of Ministry of Jal Shakti and Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs, launched in 2021. Objective: Strengthen institutional capacity, inter-city collaboration, and support Urban River Management Plans (URMPs).
evidenceBullets: Joint initiative of the Ministry of Jal Shakti and Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs launched in 2021.
Currently has 145 member cities, and any river city can join at any time.
Urban River Management Plan (URMP) Framework was launched in 2020 by NIUA and NMCG.
Chhatrapati Sambhaji Nagar’s Kham River Restoration Mission was globally recognised by the WRI Ross Centre Prize for Cities.

subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
title: Dumpsite Remediation Accelerator Programme (DRAP)
focus: SBM-U 2.0 initiative targeting legacy waste management and dumpsite remediation.
rationale: Covers critical urban waste management schemes, specific targets (Lakshya Zero Dumpsites), and environmental technologies (Biocapping, Biomining).
sectionExcerpt: It was launched under the Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban (SBM-U) 2.0. It is a year-long, targeted initiative to achieve the goals of Lakshya Zero Dumpsites by September 2026. Objective: Prioritize high-impact locations, covering ~8.8 crore MT of legacy waste.
evidenceBullets: Launched under Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban (SBM-U) 2.0 by the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA).
Aims to achieve Lakshya Zero Dumpsites by September 2026.
Prioritizes sites containing over 45,000 MT of legacy wastes, with no minimum threshold for UTs and North Eastern States.
Key technologies include Biocapping (transforming dumpsites into green zones) and Biomining (using microorganisms to extract economic materials).

subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
title: National Level Pollution Response Exercise (NATPOLREX-X)
focus: Indian Coast Guard's exercise for marine oil spill preparedness.
rationale: A specific defense and environmental response exercise, easily testable as a factual match-the-following or statement-based question.
sectionExcerpt: The Indian Coast Guard (ICG) conducted its 10th edition. A biennial flagship exercise which aims to evaluate and enhance India’s national preparedness to respond to marine oil spill incidents.
evidenceBullets: Conducted by the Indian Coast Guard (ICG).
It is a biennial flagship exercise.
Aims to evaluate and enhance India's preparedness to respond to marine oil spill incidents.

subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
title: Ground-Level Ozone (GLO)
focus: Formation mechanisms, sources, and impacts of Ground-Level Ozone.
rationale: A highly testable environmental chemistry concept. UPSC frequently asks about the precursors (NOx and VOCs) and impacts of ground-level ozone.
sectionExcerpt: Secondary, short-lived pollutant that remains in atmosphere for only hours to weeks. Created by chemical reactions between oxides of nitrogen (Nox) and volatile organic compounds (VOC).
evidenceBullets: It is a secondary, short-lived pollutant.
Created by chemical reactions between oxides of nitrogen (NOx) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs).
Natural sources include soil-based NOx emissions, wildfire-induced CO, and biospheric methane.
Safe eight-hourly ozone standard is 100 micrograms/cubic metre.
Worsens bronchitis, triggers asthma, and is a major component of smog.

subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
title: Secondary Pollutants
focus: Definition, formation, and examples of secondary atmospheric pollutants.
rationale: Core environmental science concept distinguishing primary from secondary pollutants, supported by recent CREA study findings.
sectionExcerpt: Formed in atmosphere due to chemical/physical interactions between primary pollutants themselves or between primary pollutants and other atmospheric components. Examples: Ozone (O3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3).
evidenceBullets: Account for nearly one-third of India's PM2.5 pollution according to a CREA study.
Formed by interactions between primary pollutants, sunlight, and atmospheric conditions.
Examples include Ozone (O3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), and nitric acid (HNO3).

subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
title: Toxic Heavy Metals
focus: Characteristics, sources, and specific health impacts of heavy metal pollution.
rationale: Heavy metal pollution (Lead, Arsenic, Cadmium) and their specific physiological impacts are recurring themes in UPSC Prelims.
sectionExcerpt: Heavy metals/metalloids are nondegradable and accumulate over decadal timescales in soils. Characteristics: High atomic weight ranging from 63.5 to 200.6 and density >4000 kg/m3.
evidenceBullets: Characterized by high atomic weight and density greater than 4000 kg/m3.
They are nondegradable and bioaccumulate in soils over decadal timescales.
Cadmium exposure can cause kidney and bone damage, and lung cancer.
Lead exposure causes developmental and neurobehavioral effects on fetuses.
Arsenic is highly toxic in its inorganic form and can cause skin lesions, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes.

subtopic: River Cities Alliance and Secondary Pollutants
title: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances (PFAS)
focus: Properties, applications, and environmental persistence of PFAS.
rationale: Emerging pollutants of high global concern ('Forever Chemicals'). Their chemical properties and everyday applications make them highly relevant for Science & Tech and Environment questions.
sectionExcerpt: Chemicals having carbon-fluorine bonds, which are very strong and do not degrade easily. They can resist grease, oil, water, and heat. Impact: Persists in environment (known as ‘Forever Chemicals’).
evidenceBullets: Contain very strong carbon-fluorine bonds that do not degrade easily.
Known as 'Forever Chemicals' due to their persistence in the environment.
They resist grease, oil, water, and heat.
Commonly used in cookware, food packaging, fabrics, and foams.
Researchers recently discovered nearly 20 species of bacteria capable of breaking them down.
